A dictionary of learnable parameter tensors indexed by key; users use it to store and update named weights that require gradients in a module.
A list of learnable parameter tensors that the optimizer updates; users use it to keep a variable number of trainable weights registered with the model.
A sequential container that runs layers in order from input to output; users use it to quickly stack a simple feed-forward neural network pipeline.
1D convolution layer that slides filters over a sequence to extract local patterns; users apply it to text, time-series, or signal processing tasks.
2D convolution layer that applies filters across image height and width; users use it as the core building block for CNNs in vision tasks.
3D convolution layer that scans volumetric data with filters over space and depth/time; users employ it for video analysis or 3D medical scans.
Transposed 1D convolution that upsamples a sequence by learned filtering; users use it in generators or decoders to increase temporal resolution.
Transposed 2D convolution that upsamples feature maps to larger images; users rely on it in GANs and segmentation to reconstruct high-res outputs.
Transposed 3D convolution that enlarges volumetric features; users use it in 3D generative models or video upsampling to increase spatial-temporal resolution.
Lazy 1D convolution that infers input channels from the first batch; users choose it to avoid hardcoding channel size when designing flexible models.
Lazy 2D convolution that deduces input channels on first forward pass; users use it to simplify CNN definitions when image channels aren't known early.
Lazy 3D convolution that automatically learns input channels from first data; users adopt it for volumetric networks where input depth or channels vary.
Lazy transposed 1D convolution that infers channels from first input; users use it to build sequence upsamplers without manually specifying in-channel count.
Lazy transposed 2D convolution that figures out input channels at runtime; users use it for image generators needing flexible upsampling layers.
A lazy 3D transposed convolution layer that upsamples volumetric data and infers input channels from the first batch; used to build decoders without pre-defining input size.
Computes cosine similarity between two tensors of embeddings, outputting a value from -1 to 1; used when you need to measure how alike two vectors are.
Calculates the distance between pairs of points in two batches using a chosen norm like Euclidean; used to compare matched samples in similarity or metric learning.
A dropout variant that zeroes activations while preserving mean and variance for SELU networks; used to regularize self-normalizing models without breaking their normalization properties.
Standard dropout layer that randomly sets a fraction of activations to zero during training; used to prevent neural network overfitting by reducing co-adaptation of units.
Channel-wise dropout for 1D data that drops entire feature channels per sample; used to regularize sequence or time-series models by ignoring some features completely.
Dropout for 2D feature maps that zero out whole channels per image; used to regularize convolutional nets by forcing reliance on varied feature detectors.
Dropout for 3D volumes that drops entire feature channels per sample; used to regularize 3D CNNs on video or medical scans by silencing whole feature maps.
Feature-level alpha dropout that drops whole feature planes while keeping self-normalization; used to regularize SELU networks on higher-dimensional data without distorting statistics.
Flattens multidimensional tensors into a single vector per batch sample; used to reshape conv outputs into a 1D form before feeding a dense classification layer.
Reshapes a flat 1D tensor back into a structured shape like 2D or 3D; used to convert dense layer outputs into feature maps for convolutional or unfolding steps.
Reassembles a tensor from sliding-window patches (inverse of im2col); used to reconstruct images or feature maps from extracted blocks in tasks like super-resolution.
Extracts sliding local blocks from input into column format (im2col); used to slice images into overlapping patches for custom convolution or transformer-style attention.
Normalizes each 1D sample's channels independently by mean and variance; used to stabilize training of text or audio models by removing per-sample scale differences.
Normalizes each image sample's channels separately, common in style transfer; used to decorrelate content and style by standardizing activations per individual image.
Normalizes each 3D volume sample per channel; used to apply instance-level standardization to MRI or video data for stable training of 3D convolutional networks.
Lazy 1D instance normalization that infers channel count from first forward pass; used to normalize per-sample 1D features without manually specifying input dimensions.
Lazy 2D instance normalization that infers number of channels from data; used to style-transfer or normalize images without preconfiguring the expected feature depth.
Lazy 3D instance normalization that auto-infers channels on first run; used to normalize volumetric data per sample when the input feature size is unknown upfront.
Mixin class enabling lazy modules to defer weight creation until first forward; used to build models whose layer sizes are inferred from actual input shapes.
Bilinear layer combining two input vectors via weighted interaction matrix; used when modeling pairwise relations between two inputs, like in bilinear attention or recommender systems.
Identity layer that returns its input unchanged; used as a placeholder or skip-connection that does no transformation, simplifying model wiring and experimentation.
Lazy fully-connected layer that deduces input size from first batch; used to add a dense linear transform without specifying in-features, letting shape be inferred automatically.
Standard fully-connected (dense) layer mapping inputs to outputs via weights; used to project features into class scores or hidden representations in a neural net.
Binary cross-entropy loss measuring error between probabilities and labels; used to train multi-label or binary classifiers where targets are 0/1 and outputs are sigmoid.
Binary cross-entropy with built-in sigmoid for raw logits, numerically stable; used for multi-label classification loss directly from model outputs without explicit sigmoid.
Connectionist temporal classification loss aligning variable-length sequences; used to train speech or handwriting models where transcript timing is unknown and unsegmented.
Cosine embedding loss that attracts similar pairs and repels dissimilar ones; used to train siamese networks to produce embeddings that reflect pairwise similarity.
Multi-class classification loss on raw logits combining softmax and NLL; used to train a classifier to assign each example to one of many mutually exclusive classes.
Gaussian negative log-likelihood loss for mean-variance predictions; used to train a regression model that outputs a full probability distribution instead of a single value.
HingeEmbeddingLoss computes a hinge penalty on embedding vectors to push similar pairs close and dissimilar pairs apart, used when training similarity or metric learning models.
HuberLoss calculates a regression error that behaves like squared error for small mistakes and absolute error for large ones, helping you train robust models on noisy continuous targets.
KLDivLoss measures the divergence between your predicted log-probabilities and a target probability distribution, used when you need to match a model's output to soft labels.
L1Loss computes the mean absolute difference between predicted and actual values, giving you a simple regression loss that treats all errors proportionally.
MSELoss computes the average squared difference between predictions and ground truth, the standard loss for regression tasks predicting continuous numeric values.
MarginRankingLoss scores pairs of inputs and penalizes when the first isn't higher than the second by a margin, used for learning-to-rank or preference training.
MultiLabelMarginLoss applies a hinge penalty for multi-label classification, pushing correct label scores above incorrect ones by a margin when samples have several tags.
MultiLabelSoftMarginLoss treats each label independently with a logistic loss, letting you train models that predict multiple binary tags per input simultaneously.
MultiMarginLoss computes a hinge-style penalty over class scores to keep the correct class above others by a margin, for multi-class classification training.
NLLLoss takes log-softmax class scores and returns the negative log likelihood of the true class, the standard loss for single-label classification after softmax.
PoissonNLLLoss computes negative log likelihood under a Poisson model, used when you regress on count data like event frequencies or discrete occurrences.
SmoothL1Loss combines absolute and squared error to be less sensitive near zero, often used for bounding-box regression in object detection.
SoftMarginLoss applies a logistic penalty to separate binary classes with a soft margin, used as a loss for binary support-vector-style classification.
TripletMarginLoss pulls an anchor closer to a positive sample than a negative by a margin, training embeddings for face recognition or retrieval.
TripletMarginWithDistanceLoss does triplet embedding training but uses your custom distance function instead of Euclidean, for flexible similarity learning.
register_module_backward_hook lets you attach a function that runs after a layer's backward pass, so you can inspect or change gradients for debugging.
register_module_buffer_registration_hook lets you run code whenever a buffer is added to a network, useful for tracking or modifying persistent state tensors.
register_module_forward_hook registers a callback that executes after a layer's forward call, letting you examine or tweak its output during a forward pass.
register_module_forward_pre_hook sets a callback before a layer computes its forward pass, so you can inspect or alter inputs going into the module.
register_module_full_backward_hook runs a function after all gradients for a module are computed, letting you observe or modify the full gradient flow.
register_module_full_backward_pre_hook runs before a module's full backward pass, allowing you to inspect or modify incoming gradients before they propagate.
register_module_module_registration_hook fires when a child module is added to a network, helping you track or modify submodules as the model is built.
register_module_parameter_registration_hook fires when a parameter is registered in a module, useful for custom initialization or tracking learnable weights.
GroupNorm normalizes activations within small channel groups independent of batch size, stabilizing training when you have tiny batches or variable sizes.
LayerNorm normalizes all features of a single sample, giving you batch-independent stabilization often used in transformers and recurrent networks.
LocalResponseNorm applies lateral contrast normalization across neighboring channels, used in older CNNs like AlexNet to encourage feature competition.
RMSNorm divides activations by their root-mean-square across features, a lightweight normalization alternative to LayerNorm for faster transformer training.
CircularPad1d wraps the ends of a 1D sequence around to pad it, so convolutions see periodic boundary conditions without artificial edges.
CircularPad2d pads an image by wrapping pixels around edges, useful for convolutional filters on periodic or toroidal 2D data.
CircularPad3d pads volumetric data with wrap-around edges, letting 3D convolutions treat boundaries as periodic without zero-padding artifacts.
This 1D padding layer adds a constant-value border to sequence tensors, used when you must extend text or audio data with a fixed padding value.
This 2D padding layer surrounds images with a chosen constant value, used when you need to add a fixed-color border before convolution or elsewhere.
This 3D padding layer fills volumetric data borders with a constant value, used when you must pad video or medical volumes with a fixed filler.
A 1D reflection padding layer mirrors sequence edges to pad borders, used when you want seamless boundary handling for signals without inventing new values.
A 2D reflection padding layer mirrors image borders to pad them, used when you need artifact-free edges for convolutional networks on pictures.
A 3D reflection padding layer mirrors volume boundaries to pad them, used when you need smooth borders for 3D convolutions on videos or scans.
A 1D replication padding layer copies edge values into the border, used when you want to extend sequences by repeating endpoint data.
A 2D replication padding layer repeats edge pixels into borders, used when you need to enlarge images by copying their outer rows and columns.
A 3D replication padding layer duplicates border voxels outward, used when you must extend volumetric inputs by repeating their outer faces.
A 1D zero-padding layer adds zeros around sequences, used when you need to widen inputs before convolution without affecting original values.
A 2D zero-padding layer adds zero borders to images, used when you need to preserve spatial size or pad pictures before convolutional filters.
A 3D zero-padding layer inserts zero borders into volumes, used when you must pad 3D tensors for convolution while leaving data unchanged.
PixelShuffle is an upsampling layer that rearranges channel blocks into spatial resolution, used when you want cheap high-res output after convolution.
PixelUnshuffle is a downsampling layer that folds spatial pixels into channels, used when you need to compress resolution while increasing feature depth.
Adaptive 1D average pooling outputs a fixed-length sequence by averaging, used when you need a set-size vector from variable-length input.
Adaptive 2D average pooling yields a fixed-size feature map by averaging, used when you need a constant image representation regardless of input dimensions.
Adaptive 3D average pooling produces a fixed-shape volume by averaging, used when you require constant-size output from variable 3D inputs.
Adaptive 1D max pooling outputs a fixed-length sequence by taking maxima, used when you want a set-size summary of dominant 1D features.
Adaptive 2D max pooling gives a fixed-size map by taking max values, used when you need a constant image shape highlighting strongest activations.
Adaptive 3D max pooling yields a fixed-shape volume by maxing, used when you need constant-size 3D output emphasizing peak responses.
1D average pooling shrinks sequences by averaging local windows, used when you want to downsample time-series or signal data smoothly.
2D average pooling reduces image size by averaging patches, used when you want to smooth and downsample feature maps in CNNs.
3D average pooling downsamples volumes by averaging blocks, used when you need to shrink video or scan tensors while keeping rough structure.
2D fractional max pooling downsamples images to non-integer sizes by selective maxing, used when you need flexible output dimensions beyond standard strides.
3D fractional max pooling reduces volumes to fractional shapes by maxing, used when you want arbitrary-sized 3D outputs not possible with fixed kernels.
1D LP pooling computes p-norm over windows to downsample, used when you need a generalized pooling beyond simple max or average.
2D LP pooling applies p-norm over patches to shrink images, used when you want norm-based downsampling instead of usual max or mean.
3D LP pooling uses p-norm over blocks to downsample volumes, used when you need generalized norm pooling for 3D data.
1D max pooling shrinks sequences by taking max over windows, used when you want to extract dominant features and downsample signals.
2D max pooling reduces image size by taking max over patches, used when you want to highlight salient features and downsample CNN maps.
A 3D max pooling layer that downsamples volumetric or video data by taking the largest value in sliding windows, used to shrink spatial dimensions.
An inverse 1D max pooling layer that upsamples a condensed signal back to higher resolution using saved max locations, for reconstructing time-series features.
An inverse 2D max pooling layer that enlarges feature maps by placing max values at recorded positions, used to reverse pooling on image data.
An inverse 3D max pooling layer that upsamples volumes using stored max indices, helping restore detail in video or medical scan reconstructions.
A gated recurrent unit layer that processes sequences like text or speech, returning hidden states, used when you need efficient recurrent modeling.
A single GRU cell that computes one step of a gated recurrent network from input and prior hidden state, for custom recurrent loops.
A long short-term memory layer that learns long-range dependencies in sequences, used to model time-series or language with memory gates.
A single LSTM cell computing one timestep of hidden and cell states, letting you manually unroll long short-term memory networks.
A basic recurrent neural network layer that applies tanh or ReLU over sequences, used for simple sequential prediction or feature extraction.
Base class for PyTorch recurrent layers providing shared RNN logic, used when building custom recurrent modules or extending existing ones.
A single recurrent cell that updates a hidden state for one step, used to write your own loop over timesteps.
A lookup table turning integer indices like word IDs into dense vectors, used to embed categorical tokens for neural nets.
Efficiently computes mean or sum of embedding vectors for bags of indices, used to get fixed-size representations of variable-length token sets.
A full transformer encoder-decoder model using self-attention, used for sequence-to-sequence tasks like translation where both input and output are sequences.
The decoder half of a transformer that generates output sequences step-by-step using masked attention, for autoregressive tasks like text generation.
A single transformer decoder layer with self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward sublayers, used as a building block for decoder stacks.
The encoder half of a transformer that processes input sequences into contextual representations using self-attention, for tasks like classification.
A single transformer encoder layer with self-attention and feed-forward nets, used to stack deep attention-based feature extractors.
A generic upsampling layer that resizes feature maps to larger spatial size via nearest, bilinear, or other modes, for expanding resolutions.
A 2D bilinear upsampling layer that smoothly enlarges images or feature maps, used in decoders to increase resolution without blockiness.
A 2D nearest-neighbor upsampling layer that simply replicates pixels to enlarge maps, used for fast, cheap resolution increase in networks.
A wrapper that replicates your model across multiple GPUs and syncs gradients, used to speed up training with data parallelism.
A non-trainable tensor registered in a model, like running stats, used to store persistent state that isn't updated by backprop.
A trainable tensor with gradients enabled, used as a learnable weight or bias that the optimizer updates during training.
A buffer that delays allocation until first use, used in lazy modules to avoid specifying sizes before input shape is known.
A parameter that is created without shape and filled later, used in lazy model definitions to infer dimensions from first batch.
A helper function that checks whether a parameter or buffer is still uninitialized, used when debugging lazy modules.
Clips the total gradient norm of model parameters to a threshold and returns the norm, used to stabilize training and avoid explosions.
In-place clips the overall gradient norm of your model to a max value, used to prevent exploding gradients during training.
In-place clips each gradient value to a fixed range, used to cap extreme individual gradients and keep updates stable.
This function clips the total gradient norm of your model's parameters to a max threshold, which you use during training to stop exploding gradients and keep learning stable.
This in-place utility rescales your network's gradients so their overall norm stays under a set limit, used when training deep models to prevent unstable updates from huge gradient spikes.
It clamps every gradient value to a chosen maximum absolute size in place, which you need when preventing single huge gradient entries from destabilizing your neural network training.
This in-place function scales your gradients so their total norm equals a target value, used when you want every backward step to update with a fixed gradient magnitude.
It rearranges a 2D convolution layer's weight tensor into a different memory layout like channels-last, which you call to speed up inference or training on GPUs that prefer that format.
It changes a 3D convolution kernel's weight tensor to a hardware-friendly memory format such as channels-last, used when optimizing volumetric or video models for faster GPU execution.
This helper flattens all the learnable parameters of a neural network into one long vector, which you use when treating the whole model as a single optimization variable.
It writes a flat parameter vector back into your model's layers, restoring original shapes, which you need after manipulating the vector to update weights for custom training loops.
This function merges a convolution and its batch normalization into one layer for inference, which you use to shrink the model and make predictions faster without changing outputs.
It mathematically combines convolution weights and batch norm statistics into equivalent biased kernels, used when building fused layers manually for deployment, quantization, or custom acceleration.
This utility folds a fully-connected layer and its batch norm into a single linear layer for eval, which you call to cut latency in serving pipelines with normalized dense models.
It merges the weight and bias of a linear layer with batch norm parameters to produce fused weights, used when hand-crafting efficient inference modules.
Under the fusion submodule, this merges conv and batch norm for inference, letting you compress a trained vision model into one faster layer without altering its predictions.
Also in fusion, this combines conv and batch norm weights into a single kernel, which you use to manually construct optimized layers for production inference.
From fusion, this folds a dense layer with batch norm for evaluation, helping you streamline a normalized MLP so it runs quicker when deployed for predictions.
Within fusion, this mixes linear and batch norm parameters into fused weights, used when you want to build a single efficient fully-connected layer for serving.
This computes the combined magnitude (L2 norm) of all gradients in your model, which you check to monitor training health and detect exploding or vanishing gradients.
It builds a neural network module but skips the usual weight initialization, letting you assign custom parameters or load pretrained weights without overwriting them with random values.
Under memory_format, this converts a 2D conv weight to a different tensor layout like channels-last, which you use to match GPU expectations for faster convolution ops.
Also in memory_format, it changes 3D conv weights to a hardware-friendly memory arrangement, used when tuning video or medical volume models for accelerated training.
This top-level helper flattens every parameter of a model into one vector, which you need when doing meta-learning or hypernetwork experiments that treat weights as a point.
This wraps a weight matrix to stay orthogonal after each update, which you use to enforce a norm-preserving transform in RNNs or normalize weight matrices.
It applies spectral normalization to a layer's weight, bounding its largest singular value, used in GANs to prevent mode collapse and control the Lipschitz constant.
This reparametrizes a weight as direction times magnitude, which you use to improve optimization of deep networks by decoupling scale from the learned filter direction.
This container holds several parametrizations applied to one tensor, letting you stack constraints like orthogonality and weight norm on a layer's parameters at once.
A decorator that caches a parametrization's computed tensor between steps, which you use to avoid repeated expensive math when the underlying weight hasn't changed.
This function returns whether a module has any parametrization registered, which you call when inspecting a model to see if its weights are being constrained.
It attaches a parametrization like spectral norm to a module's weight, which you use to enforce a mathematical constraint on the fly during training.
This removes registered parametrizations and bakes their effect into plain weights, which you use before exporting a model so it needs no special wrappers.
It copies parametrizations and parameters from one module to another, which you use when transferring constrained layers into a new architecture without redefining them.
Returns the original module class before any parameterization wrapper was applied, used when you need to inspect or revert a reparametrized layer.
Base class for building custom weight-pruning techniques, used when you want to define a new way to zero out or mask neural network parameters.
Pruning module that applies a mask you supply to zero selected weights, used when you already have a custom binary mask for sparsity.
A pruning method that leaves weights unchanged, used when you need a placeholder or to test pipelines without actually removing parameters.
Prunes individual weights with smallest absolute values, used when you want to sparsify a layer by removing least important connections.
Removes entire structured blocks like channels or rows by norm size, used when you need hardware-friendly sparsity in conv or linear layers.
Holds several pruning methods and applies them together, used when you want to combine multiple sparsity strategies on one layer.
Randomly removes whole structured chunks such as filters, used when you need a quick baseline or randomized sparsity experiment.
Randomly zeros individual weights regardless of magnitude, used when you want a simple sparsity baseline or regularize by random masking.
Applies your supplied binary mask to zero selected weights in a layer, used when you already computed which connections to remove.
Prunes weakest weights across all layers by a global threshold, used when you want overall model sparsity rather than per-layer limits.
Returns the module unchanged as a pruning placeholder, used when you need a pass-through in a pruning schedule or testing.
Checks whether a module has pruning masks attached, used when you need to verify if a layer has been sparsified already.
Zeros the smallest-weight entries by absolute value in a layer, used when you want to thin a network by dropping unimportant connections.
Removes whole channels or rows with low norm from a layer, used when you need compact structured sparsity for faster inference.
Randomly drops entire structured blocks like filters from a layer, used when you need a quick randomized compression experiment.
Randomly sets individual weights to zero in a layer, used when you want a trivial sparsity baseline or random mask regularization.
Strips pruning masks and bakes zeros into weights permanently, used when you finish training and want a plain sparse layer.
Removes spectral normalization wrapping from a layer, used when you want to revert a GAN or model to unconstrained weights.
Takes weight normalization off a layer so weights are used directly, used when you want to export or simplify a trained network.
Container for variable-length RNN inputs packed together, used when you feed batches of differing-length text or time-series without padding overhead.
Reverses a permutation index array, used when you need to undo the reordering of sequences after processing a packed batch.
Converts a padded tensor of sequences into a packed format, used when you train an RNN on batches of uneven-length inputs efficiently.
Stacks a list of unequal-length tensors into one packed sequence, used when you have raw varied-length samples to feed an RNN.
Turns a packed sequence back into a padded tensor with lengths, used when you need RNN outputs as normal batches for later layers.
Pads a list of varied-length tensors to equal length with zeros, used when you need a rectangular batch for embedding or RNN input.
Splits a packed sequence into a list of separate tensors, used when you want individual variable-length outputs after RNN processing.
Extracts the original unpadded tensors from a padded batch using lengths, used when you need the true sequences without filler zeros.
Builds a layer without running its weight initializer, used when you plan to load pretrained weights or custom-init manually.
Wraps a layer to constrain its weight spectral norm, used when training GANs or needing Lipschitz-bounded neural network operations.
Removes the spectral normalization reparameterization from a model layer, which a user does when they want to drop the Lipschitz constraint from a GAN or stabilized network.
Wraps a neural network layer to enforce a Lipschitz bound by dividing weights by their largest singular value, used to stabilize GAN training and prevent mode collapse.
Runs a PyTorch module's forward pass using a temporary set of weights, letting you experiment with parameter swaps without mutating the original model.
Reshapes a single flat vector of numbers into the weight tensors of a model, used when doing custom optimization or meta-learning over parameters.
Adds weight normalization to a layer by splitting its weight into direction and scale, which helps speed up convergence in tasks like audio or language modeling.
Strips the weight normalization reparameterization from a network layer, which a user does after training to simplify the model for faster inference or export.
Wraps a module with weight normalization that decouples weight magnitude from direction, used to improve training stability and learning speed in deep networks.
Disables gradient computation during a code block, used when evaluating a model or running inference to save memory and speed up execution.
Returns the coordinates of elements in a tensor that are not zero, used to find where a condition or mask is true in your data.
Computes the length or magnitude of a vector or matrix, used when you need to regularize weights or measure distance in loss functions.
Draws random samples from a normal (Gaussian) distribution with given means and standard deviations, used to add noise or initialize weights.
Compares two tensors element by element and returns a mask of where they differ, used to check prediction errors or filter mismatches.
Returns the total count of elements in a tensor, used when you need to know the size for reshaping, flattening, or allocating buffers.
Creates a new tensor filled with the value one of a given shape, used to initialize biases or build constant masks in your network.
Returns a tensor of ones matching the shape and type of an existing tensor, used to create placeholders or scaling factors without specifying dimensions.
Optimizer that applies stochastic gradient descent with an averaged weight history, used to improve convergence stability for noisy or small-batch training.
Adaptive learning rate optimizer that adjusts per-parameter based on gradient history, used to avoid manual tuning and train robustly on varying data scales.
Memory-efficient adaptive optimizer that factors second moments to reduce state size, used for training large transformers without exhausting GPU memory.
Adaptive optimizer that accumulates past squared gradients to scale learning rates, used for sparse features like NLP or recommendation embeddings.
Default adaptive momentum optimizer that combines RMSProp and SGD with momentum, used for fast convergence across most deep learning tasks.
Variant of Adam that correctly applies weight decay separate from gradients, used to improve regularization and generalization in transformers and vision models.
Adaptive optimizer using the infinity norm for the second moment, used as a stable alternative to Adam for tasks with outlier gradients.
Second-order quasi-Newton optimizer that approximates Hessian information, used for small-data scientific optimization where each step is expensive but precise.
Optimizer that applies momentum and orthogonalizes updates for matrix params, used to train large models more stably with better feature learning.
Variant of Adam that incorporates Nesterov momentum for a lookahead update, used to squeeze extra convergence speed in training deep networks.
Adds a new set of parameters with its own learning rate to an existing optimizer, used when freezing or unfreezing layers during fine-tuning.
Restores an optimizer's saved state including momentum buffers, used when resuming training from a checkpoint to continue smoothing updates.
Registers a function to run after optimizer state is loaded, used to customize or debug checkpoint restoration like scaling migrated buffers.
Registers a function to run before optimizer state is loaded, used to modify or adapt a checkpoint's buffers before they are restored.
Registers a function to run after optimizer state is collected for saving, used to alter or log the checkpoint before writing to disk.
This registers a callback that runs just before the optimizer's state is saved to a checkpoint, letting you tweak or log internal buffers when serializing training progress.
It attaches a function that executes right after the optimizer updates model weights, so you can inspect results, log metrics, or modify parameters post-step.
It adds a hook that fires before the optimizer applies a weight update, letting you adjust gradients or take actions using the current parameters and gradients.
It returns a Python dictionary of the optimizer's internal state and hyperparams, which you save and load to resume or transfer a training run.
This method performs one training step, moving model parameters using accumulated gradients and the chosen algorithm after you have run backward propagation.
This clears the gradients stored on model parameters so you can start a fresh backward pass without leftover values from the previous iteration.
RAdam is an adaptive momentum optimizer that corrects for early noisy updates, used when you want stable convergence without a separate learning-rate warmup.
RMSprop rescales each parameter's update by a running average of recent gradients, helping you train recurrent nets or handle non-stationary objectives.
Rprop updates weights using only the sign of gradients with separate step sizes, useful when you want to ignore gradient magnitude and train robustly.
SGD updates parameters opposite to the gradient, with optional momentum, and is the standard go-to optimizer for classic deep network training.
SparseAdam is an Adam variant built for sparse tensors like embeddings, letting you efficiently optimize only the nonzero indices that appear in a batch.
Adadelta is an adaptive optimizer that automatically scales learning rates per parameter using gradient history, so you avoid manual tuning when training.
This is the low-level function that computes a single Adadelta parameter update, for users who want to apply or study the raw math outside the class.
Adagrad adapts learning rates by accumulating squared gradients, making it handy for sparse input like text where infrequent features need smaller steps.
This functional version applies the Adagrad update rule directly to tensors, for when you need the core algorithm without the full optimizer wrapper.
Adam combines momentum and per-parameter adaptive learning rates, offering a reliable default optimizer that converges quickly across many model types.
This is the stateless function implementing Adam's update, letting advanced users apply the optimization math manually inside custom training loops.
Adamax is a variant of Adam using the infinity norm for steadier adaptive updates, useful when you want bounded, stable training dynamics.
This functional form performs the Adamax update on raw tensors, for users needing the bare algorithm outside the standard optimizer class.
AdamW is Adam with decoupled weight decay, the preferred optimizer for transformers and modern networks where proper regularization matters.
This function applies the AdamW update with separate weight decay, letting you use the exact decoupled algorithm in custom or research code.
ASGD averages the model weights over recent steps to improve generalization, used when you want a smoothed final model after SGD training.
This is the pure function that computes an averaged SGD step, for when you wish to implement or experiment with weight averaging directly.
LBFGS is a second-order quasi-Newton optimizer that uses gradient history to approximate curvature, suited for small-batch or full-batch convex problems.
ChainedScheduler wraps several learning-rate schedulers in sequence, so you can combine policies like warmup then cosine decay in one schedule.
ConstantLR multiplies the learning rate by a fixed factor each step, giving a simple way to hold or slightly shift lr during training.
CosineAnnealingLR lowers the learning rate along a cosine curve to near zero, providing smooth decay often used for image or NLP training.
CosineAnnealingWarmRestarts cycles the lr down and back up on a cosine schedule, helping models escape local minima by periodic restarts.
CyclicLR oscillates the learning rate between set bounds in repeated cycles, letting you actively explore the loss landscape during training.
ExponentialLR decays the learning rate by a constant gamma factor every epoch, giving steady geometric shrinkage for simpler training regimes.
Base class for learning rate schedulers that adjusts the optimizer’s step size during training; use it when building a custom schedule to decay or warm up learning rate.
LambdaLR scales your learning rate by a custom function of epoch number, letting you apply any arbitrary decay rule when standard schedulers don’t fit.
LinearLR linearly interpolates the learning rate between a start and end factor over a set number of epochs, useful for warmup or smooth linear decay.
MultiStepLR drops your learning rate by a fixed factor at manually specified epoch milestones, ideal when you want step decay at custom training checkpoints.
MultiplicativeLR multiplies the learning rate by a user-supplied factor each epoch, letting you shrink or grow the step size with a simple recurring rule.
OneCycleLR cycles the learning rate from a max to min and back across one training run, used for super-convergence and faster model training.
PolynomialLR decays the learning rate following a polynomial curve from start to end factor, handy when you need a smooth non-linear schedule.
ReduceLROnPlateau lowers the learning rate when a monitored metric like validation loss stalls, helpful for adaptive training when progress plateaus.
SequentialLR chains multiple learning rate schedulers back-to-back across training phases, so you can combine warmup with later step or cosine decay.
StepLR reduces the learning rate by a fixed gamma every constant number of epochs, giving a simple periodic decay for steady training runs.
NAdam is an optimizer combining Adam with Nesterov momentum, used when you want adaptive updates with extra acceleration for faster convergence.
The nadam functional form applies one Nesterov-Adam update step manually to parameters, useful for custom training loops outside optimizer classes.
RAdam is a rectified Adam optimizer that stabilizes early training by fixing adaptive momentum variance, good for noisy or small-batch runs.
The radam function performs a single rectified Adam parameter update, letting you hand-code optimization while avoiding the full optimizer object.
RMSprop is an adaptive optimizer that scales gradients by recent magnitude with momentum, often used to train recurrent nets more stably.
The rmsprop functional step updates parameters using RMSprop rules manually, helpful when writing custom training loops without the optimizer class.
RProp is an optimizer that updates weights using only gradient signs with separate step sizes, suited for full-batch training on smooth losses.
The rprop function applies one resilient backpropagation update step by hand, useful for low-level control outside the standard optimizer wrapper.
SGD is the classic stochastic gradient descent optimizer with optional momentum, the default choice for training neural nets from scratch.
The sgd function performs a single manual SGD update on tensors, letting you implement custom training steps without the optimizer object.
SparseAdam is an Adam variant that handles sparse gradients efficiently, ideal for updating embedding tables or large sparse feature weights.
AveragedModel wraps a network to maintain a running average of weights, used in stochastic weight averaging for better test-time generalization performance.
SWALR is a learning rate scheduler that anneals the rate linearly for stochastic weight averaging, helping final model converge to flat minima.
orgqr computes the orthogonal Q matrix from a QR decomposition of a given matrix, needed for linear algebra tasks like solving least squares.
ormqr multiplies a matrix by the orthogonal factor Q from a QR decomposition, letting you apply the rotation to another set of vectors.
outer computes the outer product of two vectors as a matrix of pairwise products, useful for covariance or rank-one update computations.
pca_lowrank computes a low-rank principal component approximation of a data matrix, letting you reduce dimensions efficiently on big datasets.
permute rearranges the dimensions of a tensor to a new axis order, something you need when switching from channels-last to channels-first layout.
pinverse computes the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse of a matrix, used to directly solve linear least-squares problems or invert non-square matrices.
poisson draws random samples from a Poisson distribution given a rate tensor, useful for generating count noise or simulating event data.
Constructs complex numbers from magnitude and angle (phase) inputs, used when you need to build complex-valued tensors for signal processing or Fourier-domain ML tasks.
Computes the polygamma function (derivative of log-gamma) for given order and inputs, used in advanced probability, custom losses, or specialized ML math needing higher-order digamma.
Creates a new tensor with the same values as input, acting as a positive identity op, useful for autograd or type handling when you need unchanged positive data.
Raises each element of a tensor to a power from another tensor or scalar, used for squaring activations, inverse transforms, or custom power-law feature scaling.
Multiplies all elements (or along a dimension) to produce a product tensor, used when you need reduced multiplication for joint probabilities or scalar aggregation.
Returns the common data type that two tensor dtypes promote to, used when mixing precisions to predict resulting dtype and avoid unexpected casting errors.
Computes QR decomposition into orthogonal Q and triangular R matrices, used to solve linear systems, orthogonalize features, or stabilize training via matrix factorization.
Calculates requested quantile values (like percentiles or median) of tensor data, used to set thresholds, summarize distributions, or clip outliers in model outputs.
Converts a float tensor to low-precision integers with separate scale and zero-point per channel, used to compress model weights for faster quantized inference.
Converts a float tensor to quantized integers using one scale and zero-point for the whole tensor, used to shrink memory of activations or weights uniformly.
Applies batch normalization directly on quantized tensors, used when you need to normalize activations in a low-precision network without costly dequantization steps.
Performs max pooling over 1D quantized tensors, used to downsample sequence or temporal data in a quantized model while keeping efficient low-precision computation.
Performs max pooling over 2D quantized tensors, used to downsample images in a quantized CNN without converting back to floating-point for each layer.
Generates low-discrepancy Sobol quasi-random sequences for efficient space-filling sampling, used in hyperparameter tuning, Monte Carlo integration, or balanced dataset splitting.
Converts angle values from radians to degrees, used when model outputs or targets are in radians but you need degrees for reporting, plotting, or standard metrics.
Draws uniformly distributed random numbers between zero and one, used for weight init, dropout masks, or generating synthetic data with equal probability.
Creates uniform random values with the same shape and dtype as a given tensor, used for noise or masks that must match another layer's size.
Samples random integers from a given range, used for drawing categorical indices, building random batches, or discrete data augmentation in training loops.
Generates random integers matching the shape of an existing tensor, used when you need discrete masks, indices, or labels with the same dimensions as another tensor.
Draws random numbers from a standard normal distribution, used for weight initialization, adding Gaussian noise, or simulating data with zero mean and unit variance.
Produces Gaussian noise with the same shape as a reference tensor, used to add matching random perturbations or initialize like an existing layer's parameters.
Generates a random permutation of integers from zero to n-1, used to shuffle dataset indices for batching or randomize order in training epochs.
Creates a tensor of evenly spaced values from start to end inclusive, used when you need a fixed sequence range, though arange is often preferred.
Flattens a tensor into a single 1D vector without copying data, used when you need to collapse dimensions for a fully-connected layer or vector ops.
Extracts the real component from complex-valued tensors, used when your model outputs complex numbers but you need real values for loss, metrics, or plotting.
Computes the element-wise reciprocal (1 divided by each value), used for inverse scaling, dividing by denominators efficiently, or building custom learning rate schedules.
Computes the element-wise remainder after division, used for cyclic indexing, wrapping array positions, or implementing modulo-based schedules and padding in neural networks.
Renormalizes slices of a tensor so each has a given norm, used to clip row or column norms of embeddings and keep gradients stable.
Repeats each element of a tensor consecutively a given number of times, used to upsample labels or expand features by duplicating entries along a dim.
Returns a view of the tensor with different dimensions but same data, used to reshape inputs to fit layer sizes like flattening or adding channels.
Returns a tensor with the conjugate bit applied, giving true complex values when you need to materialize or store an actual conjugated array.
Returns a tensor with pending negation applied, yielding the true negative values when you need to materialize or compute with the negated array.
Tells you the resulting dtype when mixing two tensors, so you can predict the output type before doing an operation like addition.
Circularly shifts elements along a chosen dimension, useful when you want to rotate or wrap array contents instead of padding or cropping.
Rotates a tensor by 90 degrees along selected planes, handy when you need to flip image orientation or spatial data without manual transposes.
Rounds each element to the nearest whole number, used when you need integer values from a float tensor for indexing or discrete steps.
Vertically stacks a sequence of tensors into a single array, so you can combine rows or batch examples along the first dimension.
Computes the reciprocal square root of each element, often used to normalize activations or scale gradients efficiently during model training.
Serializes a tensor or model to a file, letting you save checkpoints or persist trained weights for later loading.
Writes source values into a target tensor at specified indices along a dim, used to assign or rearrange data by position.
Adds source values into a target tensor at indexed positions, summing overlaps, useful for sparse accumulation or building feature histograms.
Reduces source values (sum, mean, etc.) into a tensor at indices, letting you pool or aggregate grouped elements like segments.
Finds insertion indices in a sorted tensor to keep order, used to bucketize values or map data into bins.
Sets the global random number generator seed to a fixed value, so your model initialization and data shuffling become reproducible across runs.
Reduces contiguous segments of a tensor with sum or mean, helpful when you need grouped aggregates like per-instance batch stats.
Slices out a single index along a dimension, letting you grab one row, channel, or timestep as a view.
Writes a given tensor into a chosen index along a dimension, used to replace a row, slice, or channel in place.
Sets the device for all newly created tensors, so you can default new arrays to GPU without specifying every time.
Changes the default floating point precision for new tensors, letting you default to float64 or half precision like bfloat16 globally.
Sets the default tensor type combining dtype and device, so new tensors automatically land on GPU with your chosen precision.
Configures PyTorch to enforce deterministic algorithms or error on nondeterministic ones, helping you debug reproducibility issues in model training runs.
Sets the precision mode for float32 matrix multiplies, so you can trade some accuracy for faster TF32 training on modern GPUs.
Flushes denormal (subnormal) floating point values to zero on CPU, helping you avoid slow computations with tiny near-zero numbers in loops.
Configures the number of threads used for inter-op parallelism, helping you balance CPU usage when running multiple independent torch operations.
Sets the thread count for intra-operator parallelism, so you can limit how many CPU cores work on one tensor op.
Configures the printing behavior of tensors, so you can adjust precision, threshold, and width when inspecting large model weights or outputs.
Restores the PyTorch RNG state from a saved snapshot, so you can reproduce the same random draws for debugging or training.
Configures PyTorch to show warnings on every occurrence rather than suppressing repeats, helpful when debugging repeated training issue messages.
Computes the sign of complex or real tensors as a unit magnitude value, giving the phase direction for complex math.
Applies the logistic sigmoid activation to tensor elements, converting raw scores into 0-1 probabilities for neural net binary classification or gates.
torch.sign returns the sign (-1, 0, 1) of every element in a tensor, which you need when checking positivity, thresholding gradients, or implementing sign-based optimizers.
torch.signal.windows.bartlett creates a Bartlett (triangular) window tensor for tapering signals, which you use before FFT or filter design to reduce spectral leakage.
torch.signal.windows.blackman generates a Blackman window vector for signal processing, used when you need to taper a signal before Fourier analysis to minimize edge effects.
torch.signal.windows.cosine builds a raised-cosine window tensor, which you need to smoothly fade signal edges before spectral analysis or short-time Fourier transform.
torch.signal.windows.exponential produces a decaying exponential window, used when you want to weight recent samples more heavily in time-series masking or filter design.
torch.signal.windows.gaussian creates a bell-shaped Gaussian window for smoothing or spectral analysis, which you need to localize frequency content with minimal ringing artifacts.
torch.signal.windows.general_cosine makes a custom cosine window from given coefficients, used when you need a tailored taper for specialized spectral estimation tasks.
torch.signal.windows.general_hamming returns a generalized Hamming window with adjustable parameters, used when you need flexible signal tapering beyond the standard Hamming window.
torch.signal.windows.hamming generates the standard Hamming window tensor, which you use to reduce edge discontinuities when applying FFT or convolution to signals.
torch.signal.windows.hann creates a Hann (Hanning) window for spectral analysis, used when you need to suppress leakage by tapering signal ends before transform.
torch.signal.windows.kaiser builds a Kaiser window with a shape parameter, used when you need to trade off main-lobe width and side-lobe level in filter design.
torch.signal.windows.nuttall produces a Nuttall cosine window with very low side lobes, used when you need precise frequency detection without interference.
torch.signbit returns a boolean tensor indicating whether each element is negative, which you need for masking or conditional logic based on sign without zero.
torch.sin computes the element-wise trigonometric sine, which you need for generating periodic features, rotations, or waveforms in neural network inputs.
torch.sinc evaluates the sinc function (sin(πx)/(πx)) per element, used when building interpolation kernels or ideal low-pass filters in signal processing.
torch.sinh computes the hyperbolic sine of each element, which you might need for certain activation functions, differential equations, or numerical stability transforms.
torch.slice_scatter writes a given slice tensor into a larger tensor at specified positions, used when you need to update regions without copying the whole array.
torch.slogdet computes the sign and log-determinant of square matrices, which you need for stable log-likelihood, matrix inversion, or normalizing flows computations.
torch.smm performs sparse matrix multiplication with a dense matrix, used when you need efficient linear transforms on large sparse graph or feature data.
torch.softmax computes normalized exponential probabilities from raw logits along a dimension, which you need for multi-class classification or attention weights.
torch.sort returns sorted values and their indices along a dimension, used when you need ranking, top-k selection, or ordering tensor elements for analysis.
torch.sparse.addmm combines sparse matrix multiplication with sparse addition (alpha*spmm + beta*sp), used for efficient layered updates in sparse neural nets.
torch.sparse.as_sparse_gradcheck converts a sparse tensor for gradient checking, used in tests to verify backward passes on custom sparse operations correctly.
torch.sparse.check_sparse_tensor_invariants validates that a sparse tensor's indices and values are consistent, used when debugging custom sparse format ops.
torch.sparse.log_softmax computes log-softmax over sparse tensors, used when you need stable log-probabilities for sparse classification or sequence modeling losses.
torch.sparse.mm multiplies a sparse matrix by a dense matrix, which you need for scalable graph convolutions or embedding lookups with sparse adjacency.
torch.sparse.sampled_addmm performs sparse matrix multiply and add only on sampled indices, used to efficiently compute masked updates in large sparse models.
torch.sparse.softmax computes softmax probabilities for sparse tensors, used when you need normalized attention or class scores on memory-efficient sparse inputs.
torch.sparse.spdiags builds a sparse matrix with specified diagonals from input vectors, used when constructing regularizers or linear operators without dense storage.
torch.sparse.spsolve solves a linear system with a sparse coefficient matrix, used when you need efficient solutions to large-scale scientific ML problems.
Computes the sum of non-zero entries in a sparse tensor, used when you need total or reduced values from a large sparse matrix without densifying.
Builds a block compressed sparse column tensor, used when you want efficient storage and multiplication of sparse matrices with small dense blocks like in structured ML.
Creates a block sparse row tensor for compressed block storage, used when you need fast sparse matrix multiply on GPUs with block-wise patterns.
Constructs a sparse tensor using compressed format indices, used when you have CSR/CSC data and want a unified sparse tensor for efficient ops.
Makes a coordinate-format sparse tensor from indices and values, used when you have scattered non-zeros like graph edges and want memory-efficient storage.
Builds a compressed sparse column tensor, used when you need column-oriented sparse matrix for fast column slicing or solving linear systems.
Creates a compressed sparse row tensor, used when you want row-wise sparse matrix storage for efficient multiplication or indexing in training.
Splits a tensor into multiple smaller chunks along a dimension, used when you need to divide a batch or sequence into parts for separate processing.
Computes elementwise square root of a tensor, used when you need to normalize values or compute scale from variance in your network.
Returns elementwise square of each value, used when you want to compute quadratic terms, penalties, or variance from a tensor.
Removes dimensions of size one from a tensor, used when you want to simplify shape after pooling or broadcasting to match expected layer input.
Performs sparse matrix multiplication and adds to a sparse tensor, used when you need efficient alpha*(A@B)+beta*C without densifying.
Joins a list of tensors into one by adding a new dimension, used when you want to batch separate samples into a single tensor.
Calculates standard deviation across tensor dimensions, used when you need spread or normalization statistics of activations or dataset features.
Computes standard deviation and mean together in one pass, used when you need both stats for batch normalization without extra computation.
Applies short-time Fourier transform to a signal, used when you need a spectrogram for audio classification or time-frequency feature extraction.
Subtracts one tensor from another elementwise, used when you need to compute differences, residuals, or deltas between model outputs.
Alias for elementwise subtraction, used when you want to take one tensor away from another to get error or change in predictions.
Adds up all elements or along a dim of a tensor, used when you need totals, loss sums, or reduced features for downstream tasks.
Factorizes a matrix into singular values and vectors, used when you need low-rank approximation, PCA, or covariance analysis in ML.
Computes truncated SVD for large matrices efficiently, used when you need approximate top singular components for recommender or embedding.
Swaps two specified axes of a tensor, used when you need to reorder dimensions like moving channels before height for conv layers.
Same as swapping axes, used when you want to transpose specific dimensions of a tensor to match expected layout for operations.
Marks a value as symbolic float for dynamic shapes, used when you trace models with torch.compile and need flexible floating shape math.
Creates a fresh symbolic size for dynamic tracing, used when you define new variable batch dimension in torch.compile for flexible shapes.
Treats an integer as symbolic for shape inference, used when you compile with dynamic batch size and need symbolic int in guards.
Builds a symbolic if-then-else expression, used when you need conditional dynamic shape logic while tracing models with torch.compile.
Computes symbolic maximum of symbolic dimensions, used when you need to combine dynamic shape sizes for padding or concatenation bounds.
Computes symbolic minimum of symbolic sizes, used when you need lower bound for slicing or aligning variable tensor dimensions safely.
Negates a symbolic boolean condition for dynamic shapes, used when you need logical not in shape guards while compiling with torch.compile.
Symbolic square root for dynamic shape tracing: use it when you need the sqrt of a symbolic tensor size while writing torch.compile or export code.
Symbolic sum over symbolic integers or shapes: use it when you need to add up dynamic tensor dimensions or symint values in shape calculations.
Matrix transpose that flips rows and columns of a 2D tensor: use it when you need to rotate a matrix for linear algebra or dot products.
Flat indexing getter that pulls elements by their absolute positions: use it when you need to gather specific entries from a tensor using a list of indices.
Axis-wise gather that selects values along a chosen dimension by index tensor: use it when you need to pick predictions along a specific axis.
Element-wise trigonometric tangent of angles in radians: use it when you need tan activation or periodic features in signal processing or custom layers.
Hyperbolic tangent activation that squashes numbers to between -1 and 1: use it when you need a bounded nonlinearity for RNNs or gradient stability.
Constructor that makes a PyTorch tensor from a Python list or array: use it when you need to convert your data into a tensor for model input.
Splitter that divides a tensor into multiple chunks along an axis by sections: use it when you need to break a batch into smaller parts.
Generalized tensor contraction like einsum over specified dimensions: use it when you need a multi-axis dot product or batched matrix multiply for fusion.
Repeater that tiles a tensor by copying it along dimensions: use it when you need to replicate features or expand data without copying memory heavily.
Finder of the k largest values and their indices: use it when you need top predictions, highest losses, or ranking results from a score tensor.
Sum of the diagonal entries of a 2D matrix: use it when you need total trace for matrix diagnostics or regularization penalties.
Dimension swapper that exchanges two axes of a tensor: use it when you need channels-last layout or to permute shape for convolution.
Numerical integration using the trapezoidal rule along an axis: use it when you need area under a curve or cumulative loss over time.
Legacy trapezoidal integration alias: use it when you need to compute area under a sampled curve and are on older PyTorch code.
Solver for linear systems with triangular coefficient matrix: use it when you need efficient backward substitution for Cholesky or LU factors in ML.
Lower-triangle extractor that zeros entries above the diagonal: use it when you need causal attention mask or Cholesky factor in a network.
Index generator for the lower triangular part of a matrix: use it when you need coordinates to build sparse masks or packed attention.
Upper-triangle extractor that zeros entries below the diagonal: use it when you need upper part for matrix decomposition or custom ops in ML.
Index generator for the upper triangular part of a matrix: use it when you need coordinates for sparse upper masks or pairwise distances.
True division that always returns float quotient element-wise: use it when you need standard division of integer tensors without truncation in preprocessing.
Truncation that drops fractional parts toward zero: use it when you need integer-like clipping of tensor values for indexing or buckets.
Type name inspector that returns the string name of a tensor's dtype: use it when you need to debug or log the exact type.
Dimension remover that splits a tensor into a tuple of slices: use it when you need to iterate over batch elements as separate tensors.
Shape expander that reshapes a flat dimension into multiple named dims: use it when you need to unflatten a feature vector back to spatial grid.
Unique value finder that deduplicates tensor elements: use it when you need distinct classes, labels, or to compress repeated ids in data.
Consecutive unique finder that compresses only adjacent repeats: use it when you need to reduce runs in a sequence without global dedup.
Flat-to-multi-index converter like numpy unravel: use it when you need to map linear positions back to coordinates in a multidimensional tensor for indexing.
Dimension adder that inserts a size-1 axis at a given position: use it when you need to add channel or batch dim for broadcasting.
